diabetes.name: Diabetes Risk
diabetes.description: Risk of developing type 2 diabetes based on blood sugar levels and BMI

foodsToEat: Whole grains (brown rice, quinoa, oats, whole wheat bread)
Healthy fats (avocado, olive oil, nuts, seeds)
foodsToAvoid: Sugary drinks (soda, fruit juices, energy drinks)
Refined carbohydrates (white bread, white rice, pastries)
mealTips: Eat regular, consistent meals to maintain stable blood sugar
Practice portion control - use smaller plates
Drink water before meals to reduce appetite
Include protein with every meal
Limit carbohydrate intake to 45-60 grams per meal
Choose low glycemic index foods
Avoid eating late at night
Keep a food diary to track intake

exercise: Aim for 150 minutes of moderate aerobic activity per week
Include 2-3 strength training sessions per week
Walk for 10-15 minutes after each meal
Take the stairs instead of elevators
Park farther away and walk to your destination
Do household chores actively (vacuuming, gardening)
sleep: Get 7-9 hours of quality sleep nightly
Maintain consistent sleep and wake times
Avoid screens (phone, TV) 1 hour before bed
Keep bedroom cool, dark, and quiet
stress: Try meditation or mindfulness for 10-15 minutes daily
Connect with friends and family regularly
Consider yoga or tai chi for stress relief
habits: Quit smoking - seek support if needed
Limit alcohol consumption to 1 drink per day for women, 2 for men
Monitor blood sugar levels regularly as recommended
Take medications as prescribed
Attend regular check-ups with your healthcare provider

immediate: Schedule an appointment with your healthcare provider
Purchase a blood glucose monitor if recommended
Start a food diary to track eating habits
shortTerm: Complete fasting blood sugar test and HbA1c
Schedule eye examination for diabetic retinopathy screening
Get foot examination and podiatrist consultation
longTerm: Work with registered dietitian for meal planning

high.bloodPressure: Weekly at home, at every visit

recommendations: Maintain a healthy lifestyle with regular physical activity
Continue monitoring blood sugar levels annually
Follow a balanced diet rich in fiber and whole grains
Maintain healthy body weight
Reduce sugar and refined carbohydrate intake
Increase physical activity to at least 150 minutes per week
Monitor blood sugar levels every 6 months
Consult with a healthcare provider for personalized advice
Consider lifestyle counseling programs
Maintain a food diary to track eating habits
Consult with an endocrinologist immediately
Follow a strict low-carbohydrate diet under medical supervision
Begin regular blood glucose monitoring
Consider diabetes prevention program enrollment
Increase physical activity to 300 minutes per week
Discuss medication options with your healthcare provider
Schedule regular follow-up appointments

anemia.name: Anemia Risk
anemia.description: Risk of anemia based on hemoglobin levels and demographic factors

foodsToEat: Citrus fruits (oranges, lemons) for vitamin C
foodsToAvoid: Tea and coffee (tannins inhibit iron absorption)
Dairy products in large amounts (can block iron)
mealTips: Eat iron-rich foods with vitamin C for better absorption
Cook in cast iron cookware to increase iron content
Avoid drinking tea/coffee with meals
Soak beans and legumes before cooking
Eat meat/fish with vegetables for better absorption
Consider taking iron supplements between meals
Space calcium supplements from iron-rich meals

exercise: Maintain moderate exercise routine (150 min/week)
Include strength training 2x per week
Listen to your body - fatigue is a sign to rest
Avoid intense workouts if experiencing severe fatigue
sleep: Get 8-10 hours of sleep if severely fatigued
Take short naps if needed during the day
Rest after meals to aid digestion and absorption
stress: Consider counseling if chronic stress affects appetite
habits: Avoid smoking - can worsen oxygen delivery
Limit alcohol consumption
Eat regular meals - don't skip meals
Take iron supplements as prescribed (with vitamin C)

immediate: Schedule blood test for CBC (Complete Blood Count)
Test vitamin B12 and folate levels
shortTerm: Schedule follow-up blood test in 4-6 weeks
Test for underlying causes (GI bleeding, heavy menstruation)
longTerm: Work with hematologist for chronic anemia management
Address underlying conditions (GI issues, nutritional deficiencies)
Consider IV iron therapy if oral supplements not effective
tests: Complete Blood Count (CBC) - baseline and follow-up
Iron Studies (Ferritin, TIBC, Serum Iron) - every 3-6 months
Vitamin B12 - yearly if at risk
Reticulocyte Count - to check bone marrow response
Bone Marrow Biopsy (if severe/unexplained) - as needed

recommendations: Maintain a balanced diet with iron-rich foods
Continue regular health check-ups
Include vitamin C in your diet for better iron absorption
Increase intake of iron-rich foods (leafy greens, beans, red meat)
Consider iron supplements after consulting a doctor
Ensure adequate vitamin C intake
Schedule follow-up blood tests in 3 months
Avoid tea and coffee with meals as they inhibit iron absorption
Seek immediate medical evaluation
Prescribed iron supplements may be required
Consider vitamin B12 and folate supplements
Address underlying causes (GI issues, heavy menstrual bleeding)
Schedule urgent appointment with hematologist
Follow up with complete blood count tests

vitamind.name: Vitamin D Risk
vitamind.description: Risk of vitamin D deficiency based on levels and location factors

foodsToAvoid: Highly processed foods low in nutrients
mealTips: Include fatty fish 2-3 times per week
Eat egg yolks in moderation daily
Choose fortified foods when possible
Consume vitamin D with fat-containing meals for better absorption
Consider adding cod liver oil supplement

exercise: Outdoor exercise exposes skin to sunlight naturally
Aim for 30 minutes of moderate exercise daily
Include weight-bearing exercises for bone health
Consider morning or afternoon outdoor walks
sleep: Get adequate sleep (7-9 hours) for overall health
Quality sleep supports hormone regulation including vitamin D metabolism
stress: Chronic stress can affect vitamin D absorption
habits: Get safe sun exposure (10-30 minutes daily, depending on skin type)
Use sunscreen after initial sun exposure to prevent burning
Avoid excessive use of sunscreen - blocks vitamin D synthesis
Consider vitamin D supplementation in winter months
Spend time outdoors during midday when UV rays are strongest

immediate: Get vitamin D blood test (25-hydroxyvitamin D)
Consider starting vitamin D3 supplementation (1000-2000 IU daily)
Increase sun exposure safely (10-15 minutes midday)
Add vitamin D-rich foods to diet
shortTerm: Re-test vitamin D levels in 3-6 months
Consult with healthcare provider about supplementation dosage
Consider getting bone density scan if high risk
longTerm: Consider genetic testing for vitamin D metabolism issues
Work with endocrinologist for chronic deficiency management
tests: Serum 25-Hydroxyvitamin D - baseline and follow-up every 3-6 months
Bone Mineral Density (DEXA) - if severe deficiency or at risk for osteoporosis

recommendations: Maintain current vitamin D levels through balanced diet
Continue regular sun exposure (10-15 minutes daily)
Include vitamin D-rich foods in diet
Increase sun exposure safely (15-30 minutes daily)
Consume more fatty fish, fortified dairy, and eggs
Consider vitamin D supplements (1000-2000 IU daily)
Re-test vitamin D levels in 3-6 months
Discuss supplementation with your healthcare provider
Start high-dose vitamin D supplementation immediately
Consult with a healthcare provider for proper dosage
Test for underlying conditions causing deficiency
Increase sun exposure to 30-60 minutes daily if possible
Consider calcium supplementation for bone health
Schedule follow-up tests to monitor progress

heart.name: Heart Risk
heart.description: Cardiovascular risk based on blood pressure, cholesterol, and lifestyle factors

foodsToAvoid: Saturated fats (butter, fatty meats, full-fat dairy)
High-sodium foods (canned soups, deli meats, fast food)
mealTips: Follow DASH or Mediterranean diet pattern
Use herbs and spices instead of salt for flavor
Read nutrition labels for sodium content
Limit sodium to <2300mg daily (1500mg if hypertensive)
Choose fresh or frozen vegetables over canned
Grill, bake, or steam instead of frying
Practice mindful eating - eat slowly, stop when full

exercise: Aerobic exercise: 150 minutes moderate or 75 minutes vigorous per week
Include muscle strengthening 2+ days per week
Start with 10-minute walks and gradually increase
Take activity breaks every hour when sitting
Use stairs, walk to destinations when possible
Consider cardiac rehabilitation program if recommended
sleep: Aim for 7-9 hours of sleep per night
Treat sleep apnea if suspected (snoring, daytime fatigue)
stress: Try meditation, deep breathing, or yoga
Stay connected with family and friends
habits: Quit smoking immediately - seek support programs
Limit alcohol to 1 drink/day (women), 2 drinks/day (men)
Maintain healthy BMI (18.5-24.9)
Monitor blood pressure at home regularly
Take medications as prescribed
Attend all medical appointments

immediate: Schedule appointment with primary care physician
shortTerm: Complete lipid panel (total, LDL, HDL, triglycerides)
longTerm: Work with cardiologist for ongoing management
Regular monitoring of cholesterol and blood pressure
tests: Blood Pressure - at every visit, weekly at home if high
Lipid Panel (Total, LDL, HDL, Triglycerides) - yearly
Exercise Stress Test - if symptomatic or high risk

recommendations: Maintain heart-healthy lifestyle
Continue regular physical activity
Follow a balanced diet low in saturated fats
Monitor blood pressure annually
Reduce sodium intake to less than 2300mg daily
Increase physical activity to 150 minutes per week
Follow DASH diet approach
Limit alcohol consumption
Monitor blood pressure regularly at home
Schedule annual cardiovascular check-up
Consider aspirin therapy after consulting doctor
Consult cardiologist immediately
Follow strict DASH or Mediterranean diet
Begin structured exercise program under supervision
Quit smoking if currently smoking
Consider blood pressure medication
Monitor cholesterol levels with statin therapy if prescribed
Schedule regular follow-ups with healthcare provider
Reduce stress through meditation or counseling

generalRecommendations: Stay hydrated - drink at least 8 glasses of water daily
Get 7-9 hours of quality sleep each night
Maintain a healthy weight through diet and exercise
Manage stress through meditation, yoga, or hobbies

metadata.description: Enhanced health recommendations with diet, lifestyle, actions, and monitoring schedules

vitamind.description: Parameter-specific recommendations for Vitamin D based on individual values

recommendations: ⚠️ SEVERE Vitamin D Deficiency detected (< 10 ng/mL)
Consult a healthcare provider immediately for high-dose supplementation
Consider Vitamin D3 supplementation of 4000-10000 IU daily under medical supervision
Test calcium, phosphorus, and PTH levels to check for secondary hyperparathyroidism
Increase sun exposure to 30-60 minutes daily (midday, with exposed skin)
Include fatty fish (salmon, mackerel) in your diet 3 times per week
⚠️ Vitamin D Insufficiency detected (10-20 ng/mL)
Consider Vitamin D3 supplementation (2000-4000 IU daily)
Increase sun exposure to 20-30 minutes daily
Add more Vitamin D-rich foods: fortified dairy, egg yolks, fatty fish
Re-test Vitamin D levels in 3 months to monitor progress
📊 Vitamin D is in the insufficient range (20-30 ng/mL)
Consider Vitamin D3 supplementation (1000-2000 IU daily)
Aim for 15-20 minutes of daily sun exposure
Include Vitamin D-rich foods in your diet regularly
✅ Vitamin D levels are within normal range
Maintain current Vitamin D levels with balanced diet
Continue regular sun exposure (10-15 minutes daily)
Consider annual Vitamin D testing if at risk
☀️ Very limited sun exposure detected
Consider Vitamin D supplementation especially in winter months
Try to get outdoor time during midday (10 AM - 3 PM)
Even 15-20 minutes of sunlight can help synthesize Vitamin D
☀️ Limited sun exposure - consider Vitamin D supplements
Try to increase outdoor time, especially in morning or midday
☀️ Good sun exposure - helps maintain Vitamin D levels
Remember to use sunscreen after initial exposure to prevent burning
⚠️ Obesity (BMI ≥ 30) can affect Vitamin D metabolism
Higher Vitamin D doses may be needed due to fat sequestration
Consult with healthcare provider about appropriate supplementation
Consider getting Vitamin D levels tested regularly
📊 Overweight (BMI 25-29.9) may require slightly higher Vitamin D
Maintain healthy diet and exercise routine
⚠️ Age > 65: Skin's ability to produce Vitamin D decreases
Consider Vitamin D supplementation (1500-2000 IU daily)
Increase dietary intake of Vitamin D-rich foods
Regular testing recommended
📊 Age > 50: Vitamin D production decreases with age
Consider annual Vitamin D testing
⚠️ Heavy alcohol consumption affects Vitamin D metabolism
Consider higher Vitamin D supplementation under medical guidance
Limit alcohol intake for better nutrient absorption
Get liver function tests if consuming alcohol regularly
📊 Moderate alcohol consumption may affect Vitamin D
Consider monitoring Vitamin D levels
🏃 No regular exercise detected
Consider outdoor exercise to boost Vitamin D from sunlight
Start with 15-30 minute walks daily
⚠️ Very low iron intake (< 50% RDA) can affect Vitamin D metabolism
Iron deficiency may impair Vitamin D activation
Consider iron supplementation after testing ferritin levels
Eat iron-rich foods: red meat, beans, leafy greens
📊 Low iron intake may affect Vitamin D metabolism
Consider iron-rich foods or supplementation
⚠️ Low hemoglobin (< 10 g/dL) may indicate anemia affecting overall health
Anemia can worsen fatigue and weakness
Consult healthcare provider for anemia testing
📊 Mildly low hemoglobin - consider iron-rich foods
Get complete blood count (CBC) if persistent fatigue

diabetes.description: Parameter-specific recommendations for Diabetes based on individual values

recommendations: ⚠️ CRITICAL: Fasting blood sugar ≥ 126 mg/dL indicates diabetes
Consult an endocrinologist immediately
Start blood glucose monitoring immediately
Begin low-carbohydrate diet under medical supervision
Consider diabetes medication if prescribed
⚠️ Prediabetes detected (Fasting blood sugar 100-125 mg/dL)
Act now to prevent progression to diabetes
Reduce sugar and refined carbohydrate intake
Increase physical activity to 150+ minutes per week
Lose 5-10% body weight if overweight
Schedule fasting blood sugar and HbA1c tests
✅ Fasting blood sugar is in normal range (70-99 mg/dL)
Maintain healthy lifestyle to keep levels stable
Continue regular exercise and balanced diet
⚠️ HbA1c ≥ 6.5% indicates diabetes diagnosis
Consult healthcare provider immediately
Begin regular diabetes management
Monitor blood sugar levels multiple times daily
⚠️ Prediabetes HbA1c range (5.7-6.4%)
Implement lifestyle modifications immediately
Increase physical activity
Follow low-glycemic index diet
Schedule follow-up HbA1c test in 6 months
✅ HbA1c is in normal range (< 5.7%)
Maintain healthy lifestyle habits
Continue annual screening
⚠️ Obesity Class II/III (BMI ≥ 35) significantly increases diabetes risk
Weight loss of 10-15% can dramatically reduce diabetes risk
Consult with dietitian for personalized meal plan
Consider bariatric consultation if other methods fail
Increase physical activity to 300 minutes per week
⚠️ Obesity Class I (BMI 30-34.9) increases diabetes risk
Aim for 7-10% weight loss
Follow low-calorie, low-carbohydrate diet
Increase physical activity to 150+ minutes per week
📊 Overweight (BMI 25-29.9) - consider lifestyle modifications
Maintain calorie deficit through diet and exercise
Focus on whole foods and portion control
✅ BMI is in healthy range - maintain current weight
Continue regular physical activity
Follow balanced diet
⚠️ Sedentary lifestyle is a major diabetes risk factor
Start with 10-minute walks after each meal
Aim for 150 minutes of moderate exercise per week
Use standing desk if possible
Take activity breaks every hour
📊 Occasional physical activity - increase consistency
Try to exercise at least 3-4 times per week
Add strength training 2 times per week
✅ Good physical activity level
Maintain 150 minutes of weekly exercise
✅ Excellent physical activity - continue maintaining
⚠️ Age > 60 increases diabetes risk
Regular diabetes screening recommended
Maintain healthy weight and active lifestyle
📊 Age 45-60 - start regular diabetes screening if not done
Screen at least every 3 years
⚠️ Smoking increases diabetes risk and complications
Quit smoking to reduce diabetes risk significantly
Seek smoking cessation programs or nicotine replacement therapy
Consult healthcare provider for support
⚠️ High blood pressure (≥ 140 mmHg) - common with diabetes
Monitor blood pressure regularly
Reduce sodium intake to < 1500 mg/day
Consult healthcare provider about blood pressure management
📊 Elevated blood pressure - monitor regularly
Reduce sodium intake
Increase physical activity
⚠️ High cholesterol (≥ 240 mg/dL) increases cardiovascular risk
Discuss cholesterol management with healthcare provider
Follow heart-healthy diet low in saturated fats
Consider lipid panel test
📊 Borderline cholesterol - monitor and modify diet
Reduce saturated fat intake
Increase fiber intake
⚠️ Low HDL cholesterol (< 40 mg/dL) increases diabetes risk
Increase aerobic exercise
Consume healthy fats (olive oil, nuts, avocados)
Avoid trans fats
Consider omega-3 supplementation
